author: Sofia Martínez
title: Craft Brewery Local SEO: Turn Taproom Foot Traffic Into Revenue
description: Rank your brewery higher on Google Maps, capture beer-lovers in your area, and fill events using local SEO and Google Business tactics that actually move kegs.

sections.p: Most craft breweries treat their online presence like a side pour—poorly executed and out of focus. We've worked with 40+ breweries and found that the ones generating 3x more taproom visits from search do two things differently: they dominate Google Maps with consistent local data, and they use their events as content and ranking signals. If you're not on page one of "breweries near me" or "craft beer [your city]," you're losing customers to competitors who are. Here's how to fix that.

sections.h2: Google Business Profile Optimization for Breweries

sections.p: Your Google Business Profile is your most important asset for local visibility. We see breweries get 25-40% of their web traffic from Google Maps because locals search "brewery near me" right before heading out. Start with the basics: add all product categories (brewpub, restaurant, bar), upload 10-15 high-quality photos of your taproom, beers, and seating areas monthly, and keep your hours and phone number accurate. But most breweries stop there.

Go deeper. Add your beer menu, brewhouse tours, food partnerships, and live music schedule as separate business attributes in your profile. Update your description to include your flagship beers by name—"Hazy IPA," "Barrel-Aged Stout"—so you show up in searches for specific beer styles. We tested this with a Portland brewery and saw their "hazy IPA near me" queries jump 180% in 90 days. Add posts about events, new releases, and limited-time brews every 5-7 days. Google Business posts get indexed quickly and signal active, current information.

list: Add tasting room photos monthly (at least 1-2 new images per week)
Use 8-12 local business attributes (taproom, restaurant, live music, tours, food menu partnership)
Post about events, new beer drops, and seasonal releases every 5-7 days
Ask customers to leave reviews with specific beer or dish names (triggers local search signals)
Add accurate menus, hours, and pricing to reduce bounce rate

sections.h2: Events as SEO and Traffic Drivers

sections.p: Craft brewery events—trivia nights, comedy shows, food pairing dinners, seasonal releases—aren't just revenue plays. They're also your best local SEO content. Every event you host should be documented in three places: your website (event landing page), Google Business Profile (posts and event schema), and a local event listing (Eventbrite, Meetup, Yelp Events). A brewery in Austin we worked with added 12 monthly events to their website and Eventbrite, structured the data properly, and saw their organic event-page traffic grow 320% in 6 months. These event pages rank for long-tail searches like "live music breweries Austin" or "beer tasting events near me."

Create dedicated landing pages for recurring events with specific dates, menu pairings, and guest information. Use schema markup (EventSchema) so search engines understand the event date, location, price, and performer. Include the event in your metadata title and description. Link internally from your homepage and beer menu pages to upcoming events. We've seen event-driven pages generate 15-30% of total organic traffic for breweries because they target high-intent, local, and often high-spend customers (food pairing dinners, wedding room rentals).

sections.quote: The breweries winning on local search aren't just doing SEO—they're turning their taproom schedule into digital content. Every event is a landing page, a Google Business post, and a local listing all at once.

sections.p: You need citations on Yelp, Apple Maps, TripAdvisor, and Untappd (the beer-specific platform). Untappd is critical—many beer drinkers check it before visiting. Ensure your NAP (name, address, phone number) is consistent across all platforms. A brewery in Denver had inconsistent phone numbers and missing zip codes on 5 citations, which confused Google's local algorithm. Fixing those inconsistencies plus Untappd optimization took 30 days and their Maps ranking jumped from position 8 to position 3 for "breweries Denver."

Reviews are ranking factors. Target 1-2 new reviews per week by asking customers directly in your taproom or via email. Respond to all reviews—positive and negative—within 48 hours. Mention specific beers, food items, or events in your responses. A Spokane brewery we worked with responded to 40+ reviews in 90 days, mentioning seasonal releases and upcoming events. Their review volume grew 85% and their Maps position improved because Google sees engagement and current activity.

sections.h2: Paid Search and Local Ads for Events

sections.p: Don't rely on organic alone. Run local Google Ads campaigns targeting "brewery near me," "[beer style] near me," and branded competitor searches like "Dogfish Head near me." Budget $500-1,500 monthly depending on your market size. Create separate ad campaigns for events, new releases, and general taproom traffic. We ran a campaign for a Nashville brewery with $800/month budget: they spent $250 on general taproom ads (2% conversion rate to visits) and $550 on event ads like "live music brewery Nashville this weekend" (4% conversion rate because intent was higher). They tracked foot traffic with Google Ads conversion tracking and adjusted spend accordingly.
